title: Chris Andrew - Scrunch <> GrowthX
meeting_title: Chris Andrew / Kirkland Gee - Scrunch <> GrowthX

transcript.text: Hey, Jason.
How are you?
I'm pretty good.
I can't quite hear you.
You're kind of like breaking up a little bit.
Yeah, that's running out of battery.
How is it?
Is it better now?
Yes, much actually.
Okay, great.
It's like Bluetooth is just really bad.
Like with this headset, you have to get this weird dongle and I don't know what, maybe they use some other protocol.
It's crazy to me that Bluetooth, that we don't have something better at this point, you know?
Yeah, I get it.
How's your week been?
Good.
Yeah, just, I mean, most of this week was Looker the first half and now we're...
All this PSEO stuff, but making good progress.
Hey, Chris.
How are you?
Good.
Good to see you again.
Nice to see you as well.
Nice to meet you, Jason.
Hi, Chris.
I work on clients here and go to market and growth.
Awesome.
Growthx.
Growthx.
Growthx.
What a great world to have at Growthx.
Yeah, exactly.
I assume you're critical.
Very.
Hi.
I'm Bridget.
But I run the ops team at Growthx.
And then I used to work in partnerships, actually.
So I'd mentioned that to Jason and he was like, oh, you should join these kind of calls.
So here I am.
I was just watching you on a podcast like 30 seconds ago.
So it's kind of funny to see your face right now.
The whole new speaking publicly is a thing for me.
So thanks for listening.
It's like it's a necessity.
It was like a travel experience that's travel that one.
So I was kind of confused by the title.
It's just some kind of travel.
Yeah, they were a random reach out, but they've actually been a great partner.
Nice.
Taking those opportunities.
Try to get your reps in, yep.
Exactly.
Cool.
Awesome.
Yeah, this will be everybody from our side for today, Chris.
So I appreciate you just taking a kind of explore what we could potentially do here.
Yeah.
I don't know if maybe you want us to start kind of explaining what we're trying to do, or if you kind of want to start on your side, I don't know what's easiest.
Yeah, why don't you just give me like a couple minutes on what you're trying to do.
You mentioned you're hearing kind of needs and asks from across the customer base.
I've looked a little bit at the company, I know the category, but maybe tell me a little bit about the business and kind of scale and what you're looking to solve for.
And then I can talk through what we're doing and also kind of what we're doing in this growing niche of kind of being a data provider for partners and growth players and programmatic SEO shops.
So, yeah, why we start there?
Yeah, you want me to take it away, Kirkland?
Yeah, do.
I think you probably can answer that best.
Okay, cool.
So, I mean, it looks like you looked at GrowthX ready.
Basically, you know, you can view us as like a growth agency on steroids.
You know, we combine kind of under the hood, a lot of AI and a lot of people.
And the point is like customers pay for an outcome.
We just figure it out.
And probably the biggest, you know, by far the biggest thing we do for customers today is around SEO.
And all of them are asking us, you know, what are you guys doing around AI, SEO?
I would say that the stage of our customers are all over the place.
Some of them use you guys.
Some of them use Profound.
Some of them like build stuff themselves.

And then on our end, I think it's just really important to offer the ability to track kind of clear direction on what we're doing to actually like get ahead of this, you know, to help them be cited more.
And then increasingly so, at least in our view, like distribution.
Distribution to get your content so you're not just talking about yourself is, like, critical as well.
And, you know, like, all these things, we want to kind of package together so we can help our customers, basically.
What you mean by distribution?
Kind of like, so we create a lot of content.

And then, you know, distribution for us means, like, you know, repurposing for socials, like, trying to get them mentioned in other places, getting, I mean, you know, backlinks, but not maybe in the traditional sense, just paying for links.
But having kind of very organic outreach to get mentions, because it seems like that, you know, at least for citations happens a lot.

feel like we've been seeing a little bit, like, if you just want to talk about yourself, like, I've seen a lot of instances where, like, that just doesn't really get included, even if you're ranked, you know, quite high.
Yeah.
Yeah, so I guess that's kind of our need.
Yeah, I mean, played around a bunch with Profound.
I think I've just.
Super curious, like, your view, and then what you guys are doing, and then how we can work together, basically.
Yeah.
You know.
Yeah, so if you, are you guys' customers of Profound today?
You mentioned you've played around with it.
No, we aren't, but, like, our customers are.
Yeah, our customers are customers of.
They'll just let us in, and we can help manage it in a lot of cases.
Yeah, nice.

And I guess, just to think about, I mean, if you could wave a magic wand, you'd have, like, a solution to this for your client base, I can speculate on what I think that looks like based on what I'm seeing in the market, but maybe articulate what your ideal outcome would be.

I might be able to speak to that a little bit, because eventually, probably within the next three months-ish, we'll probably have our own sort of proprietary performance reporting dashboards built into some other software that we're building.
Yeah.
Trying to make that all kind of one place.
Three months may be aggressive.
It may be longer than that before we have our own custom solution.
Yeah.
But the idea would be the same place they're looking at their GSC data, the same place they're looking at everything else.
You have a kind of all-in-one dashboard that has the context of all the work we're doing and allows them to understand the impact of that.
Not just on performance, but also on kind of the AI side of things, from citations all the way through attribution.
Right.
Yes, we were mentioned this much in AI, and then we had this many referral visits, and then that many blah, blah, blah, blah.
That's like the eventual goal right now that probably looks like Looker Studio in the meantime.
But...
Gotcha.
And I assume on the attribution side, you're already tracking that today somehow through Google Analytics, Looker, Adobe.
Yeah, depending on the client, but typically just referral visits in GA is going to be the most common.
Gotcha.
Cool.
Yeah, great, great background.
So quick background on me and the business.
I'm Chris Andrew, co-founder and CEO for Scrunch.
We started the company like 18 months ago, kind of before any of these platforms had websites.
I was like, this is where the world's going.
Humans want answers, not links, blah, blah, blah.
Started building web search.
It's been manic the last kind of four or five months.
Obviously super busy category right now, like profound.
I think they came out in maybe October of last year.
We announced maybe a month and a half after.
And then I think there's been 30 players that have jumped into the space.
So I've never really participated in a market like this.
My overall view is that monitoring is a commodity.
I'll be very clear about that.
I think every business needs to understand their presence, their position, their sentiment, their citations, track that in a very granular way over time.
We are using monitoring as a wedge in, and we're building out a number of other capabilities that we think are going to be more differentiated and valuable.
I think monitoring is going to be a super interesting, valuable standalone business, but there's more.
With that in mind, we've been growing in the market.
So we've got your traditional like Fortune 500 enterprise SaaS deals that are like.
50K to 100K a year.
And then we've got more mid-market deals that are month-to-month.
So just trying to be really accessible, get you in with like a month-to-month package.
Could be $300 a month, could be $1,000 a month based on volume of what you need.
And I'll show you the platform here in just a second.

We've carved out a second kind of line of business, if you will, in this fledgling world of basically being the data provider for folks like yourselves, where in some cases we end up overlapping with clients.
Like I've got some partners where like the partner, another growth agency, is paying for a feed of data for their client.
And their client is also paying for a login of our product.
So they have direct access and a little bit more hands-on control.
I think that's going to happen in this space.
And like, honestly, I'm fine with it.
And then there are some companies that fully outsource to folks like yourselves, and they'll never buy technology.
And obviously that's a perfect partnership situation for us because
Zero competitive overlap, and you guys should be enabled to do everything on their behalf.
I think what I'm seeing in the market is, you know, we're working with probably 60 plus companies now, like having started selling in late or January, basically.
I think we had one customer in December and now it's like gotten pretty crazy and a mix of mid-market and enterprise.
And then I think of this kind of data effort as a little bit separate from what we're doing.
For our data partners, we're kind of looking at an API.
A lot of them are using Looker, like a Looker Studio instance to take that data in, but you can take it in whatever format you like.
There's kind of four key data points that most organizations are taking, which are presence, position, and by position, we kind of average it.
We're like, are they on the top of the results, the middle of the results, the bottom of the results, if they're present at all?
Sentiment towards the organization, and then comprehensive citations.
for the things that, for the themes you're monitoring, for...
Branded and non-branded prompts, what are the sites that are being cited in the answers?
And this is covering GPT, perplexity, AI overviews, Meta, Claude, without web search right now, we haven't turned on web search for Claude, and then Gemini is going live in May.
So comprehensive coverage across the models.
Some data partners are like, look, GPT represents 90% of this.
Let's just get started with that.
And, you know, we're doing this with a number of companies.
Daydream has announced publicly, so I'll just mention that.
They're kind of like, look, we have AI search covered for our companies.
Like, we're just baking it into every contract.
And, you as you described, it's like, let's just get this embedded into core dashboards so that we have an answer and a story.
And then on the other end, you're looking at the insights and actioning gaps.
You're like, look, we need to create content or, from a distribution perspective, get our content in other places because AI is no longer just looking at my website.
We're looking at 10 sources to decide whether to mention me or not.
So maybe that.
It gives you a little sense of what we're doing.
I'm happy to give a quick walkthrough of the interface product, if that's helpful.
I mean, maybe just one question.
You said the monitoring is kind of a wedge.
For you guys, what does that mean?
Where are you guys building more into?
Yeah, you'll see a little bit of it in the product, but I think the goal for us isn't to be the rush of the future.
I think companies are going to need a completely different marketing stack to successfully operate in a world where humans are not visiting websites.
And so I think what that gets into is a little bit more infrastructure to manage crawlers and agents.
To be like, okay, these GPT users are arriving at my website.
What are they looking for?
Give it to them.
Like, they should not have access to this content, so we're experimenting in that category.
We're doing a bit of experimentation in, you know, probably similar areas to you.
think there's going to be a lot of overlap, like cooperation, cooperatition.
Well, where it's like, look, we've written all of this content for Google or humans, and we should be writing content for AI crawlers and LLMs.
Like, what's the way that an LLM retrieves content?
What is it prioritizing?
What is it looking at?
Looking at huge swaths of data to make sense of the content that needs to be created if that's the primary consumer.
So you can imagine us also participating in that down the road, which I think is good to be clear about because we're never going to be a growth end-to-end platform.
But we may have organizations think these are pages and assets that need to exist.
I think content creation is also kind of a commodity in the AI world, but, like, what goes into it based on insights is differentiated, right?
And that's probably why you guys win.
Like, you know what you're creating at scale.
I mean, we, in an AI segment, have some of those insights as well, and we'll probably look at creating in that bucket.
So all of that requires some sense of monitoring to understand how you're performing and what you can do about it.
But I think there's more interesting things to be building on top of it.
I think that makes sense.
And then, um...
I don't want to ask all the questions, but one more.

I guess the one change in thinking for this citation mentioned versus SEMrush's traditional keywords, with keywords, you're kind of aware of the universe a little bit, but with this one, there's kind of an art and a judgment to just picking, I don't know how many prompts a typical customer tracks, but 500 or 1,000, what those are.
Like, where do you think that goes?
Like, are you always kind of just like almost half guessing at the intents?
I mean, I guess you get some clues from search, right?
What the intents are, but.
Yeah, it's a great question.
I think like, got a million thoughts on it.
You probably spend an hour just on this.
I we built a prompt generation platform that looks at a ton of inputs.
So we're looking at your expected third party data input.
So Google AdWord keyword data that's publicly available.
Now, the traditional Google search is three to four words.
The traditional prompt is like 40 to 70 words.
It's kind of crazy how different that is.
I've seen some very elementary attempts by consultants and other vendors in the space to be like, here's your volume of AI search activity.
And it's like, take Google search volume and divide it by 95%.
It's like, I actually don't think that's representative or indicative of what's happening on GPT.
The other thing I've seen people do is you buy clickstream data, panel data from the likes of SimilarWeb or Datos, and you try to multiply it out, right?
You're like, hey, we have some clickstream data of like what people are doing on GPT or perplexity, people who are paid to share their browser history or incentivized to download an app.
And then you can view all of their activity on the web.
We've purchased those data sets.

My view of them is that they're really, really misleading because they're heavily VPN users coming from like India, China, people who are paid to share browser history or downloaded an app without knowing they're sharing everything on their device.
They're rarely representative of a buyer, right?
So like I'm seeing...
You can either triangulate based on traditional search.
You can buy third-party clickstream data and try to multiply it out.
And we've purchased the data sets and looked at both.
And I would not feel confident about representing a volume to any buyer that is dictating significant strategy.

What we are doing is taking that input plus a ton of other inputs, our own customer interview data, like what our prompt structure is looking like, as much third-party data as we can, and generating prompts at scale for our customers based on themes.
Personas are a huge part of our platform.
Like who does, I mean, name a customer for me.
Like give me an example of somebody you guys work with.
You know, Webflow.
Webflow.
Love the Webflow team.
Brian, one of the founders, is actually an investor in Scrunch, a good old friend of mine.
I know Vlad very well.
We started our career at Intuit together.
So them, right, they're like, who do they sell to?
Well, they're trying to move from selling to the scrappy contractor design agency to a
Fortune 500 IT team.
Those are two very different buyers who are asking very different questions and getting very different answers.
And so our platform at the core has this persona concept where you're actually mapping prompts to personas and seeing the variation and the answers that are coming back.
And so I'll show you the product in a sec, but you're going to filter by personas.
You're going to filter by stages of the customer journey, like advice and awareness.
People are not asking about growthx, but they're struggling with leads, right?
Do you guys show up when people are struggling with leads?
Obviously, you're going to show up when it's a comparison prompt of like growthx versus this.
One of the things I think that's weird about this space is I definitely see vendors gaining outcomes because depending on the prompt you ask, you can make a brand show up or not.
And so it's like, is your goal is actually conversion, like attribution, leads, outcomes.
If all you want to do is make someone feel good about their presence in AI search, just ask different prompts and you can game it.
And so I think.
We've actually seen some companies switch to our platform in the last month from other leading players, and they're just like, whoa, like, I thought we were here in AI search.
But when I went back and actually looked, I couldn't create my own prompts.
And so I'm looking at, like, category prompts, industry prompts, branded prompts, and it was way out of whack in terms of, like, how we're actually showing up.
So I think it's just super early in this space, and you need a lot of granularity in that creation process.
Cool.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
Any other questions?
No.
mean, I guess if we could look at a quick preview.
And I'm curious, have you guys explored building this yourselves?
Have you talked to anybody else that can help you provide data?
Not really.
I mean, I don't think we're really planning to build it ourselves.
I guess so.
Yeah, we briefly talked about it.
I mean, to your point...
The monitoring piece of it would not be, like, an incredibly difficult thing.
There's the simple of, like, hey, to track our presence day over day for X number of prompts per client.
But it's, like, for us, there's probably a lot of value in finding a data provider.
Just the time savings, you know, it's, like, that's the judgment call we're making.
Yep, makes sense.
Makes sense.
And then just from a size perspective, like, I don't know if you're public with, like, your client base or how many folks you work with.
Like, what's kind of broad direction of size of growthx?
Sorry, Bridget.
You mean number of customers?
Yeah, number of customers.
Oh, like 50?
Yeah.
Yeah, we're probably seeing all that public in our revenue is public because Marcel likes to build in public.
Oh, okay, cool.
I didn't make it that far in my research, but take a peek.
Just check his LinkedIn.
Okay, cool.
The will be revealed.
Thank
Yeah, it will be revealed.
There was a phase where that was very popular to do, but it's cool that people that stick with it.
Okay, let me just flip through a quick demo.
I'm just going to show some product.
Obviously, we won't demo the API here.
Let's see here.
And that's one question.
How do you think about this API product where you're offering your data like this?
Is this something you're experimenting with or something you're confident?
You know, it's like how you actually want to build out this company.
Yeah, we're confident in the offering that we are currently providing to a number of partners, like how much further that API expands, I think will be in question.
But like the monitoring piece of like, let's get data to people that are, I mean, maybe there's a world where we make it directly publicly available and competitors can build directly on top of it.
But like, actually, I think given the direction of the space, that's what like a lot of the...
I mean, SEMrush does that in its own way right now.
And so that could make sense.
But again, I think the more interesting business is what you do on top of the monitoring platform.
So, yes.
So then, like, how do you see, like, the value of, like, giving this stream away?
Because it's like, I mean, it's somewhat valuable, right?
Like, that infrastructure for you to keep up.
Is it, I mean, just like, how many dollars I'm paying you for this API?
Or, like, do you want it to be kind of some play where, like, you know, and we're open to it, like, for our customers to, like, get into your product and buy more?
Yeah, that, I mean, our other partner agreements have that baked in.
So we have kind of like a mutual referral situation where it's like, if we send a customer to you guys, X cut of it.
If you send a customer to us, X cut of it.
And I think that becomes more interesting as we add more product capabilities on top.
I mean, I kind of have a thesis that, like, I don't know the timing of it.
But I think the web is going to look entirely different.
Like if 95% of content is for AI crawlers and agents taking outcomes, like everything's going to look different.
But I think as we build more capabilities that direction, the referral opportunity, vice versa, gets more powerful.
So that is a core part of it.
And then I think we've built this out for ourselves.
It makes sense to monetize it with partners that are a good fit to scale and have a need.
And it's kind of our core competency.
Like our background is enterprise data.
My co-founder is like a deep data person.
already came from Quantcast.
And our prior company, we only sold to the top 500 banks and insurance companies.
was huge enterprise API integrations, just like a ton of controls.
And we're very competent in that category.
So it's like, okay, I think a lot of people are doing this in a pretty elementary or isolated way.
We have confidence in our ability to scale it and like have it be a core part of our offering.
So if part
The question too is like, are you experimenting with this and you're going to shut it down in a year like that?
We haven't been talking about it as an experiment.
We've been talking about it as like, this is a core capability we need to for business and we have significant partners that are relying on it.
I know the space is super new and I mean, who knows, right?
Like same with us.
I think like where we want to be is like, like we're kind of tool agnostic.
Yep.
Like my view, our view is like, for you to be able to do all this stuff, you kind of need an incredible team that not every company has or even wants to build and that's why they come to us.
So the partnerships you have today, the ones that it's just like a data feed, are you, do you have a, are you charging on the customers or the volume or what are, what's it?
Yeah, we've been charging on customer, like customers and volume.
Like I will say it like, say again.
Like both dimensions?
I mean, essentially it breaks down to the volume of things you're monitoring, but we have been charging on both dimensions of like, okay, you're bringing in 20 entities.
Times monitoring 300 or 500 prompts on the number of models that you want to cover.
And we've been doing kind of custom pricing based on that.
Okay.
Yeah.
And I'd say that significantly ranges.
Like some companies come in and they're like, we need a super high volume of prompts and we need it across all models.
And then others come in and say, look, like the reality is like the answers to these prompts are not changing significantly.
And so like we don't need to be capturing at a crazy frequency.
We only want one or two models.
And so right now I think we don't have like a formulaic pricing approach for this.
Like this has been like a back and forth based on company need to find something that that works as we continue to do it.
Okay.
But if someone works directly with you, it's basically on, it's just volume.
There's no like flat fees or something or platform fees.
It's just like the volume of prompts times.
Like.
A company just signs up for the product, you mean?
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's a general pricing model.
I mean, then typically there's just one brand, right?

Like, Skims, Lenovo, like, they sign up as Skims to monitor X thousand prompts, and they've got their users and their personas and their competitors, and they're paying an annual platform fee to use it.
Okay.
Is your pricing public on the website?
It's not.
Okay.
Not yet.
Not yet.
Probably will be in the coming weeks.
Okay.
Let's just quickly flip through this.
I got a hard stop at nine.
So I'm just, I'm in a, it's a live environment to demo work.
So Disney Plus is the pretend, pretend you're helping Disney Plus drive growth.
Just to give you a quick sense of what we do, we simply take the brand name.
We start generating a bunch of information automatically.
So a description of what they do.
Personas are built out automatically of the types of people Disney Plus sells to.
So you see, like, the third parent versus a nostalgic pop culture fan.
These are, these folks have different prompts, different answers coming back.
So part of what we build out with our customers is who are they selling to?
What type of things are they asking?
What are the answers coming back?
Competitive brands gets auto mapped.
Key topics and themes, think of these as like indicative of traditional search that helps translate into our prompt generation platform things that you actually want to be monitoring.
And this starts generating prompts at scale.
Companies can come in bulk upload prompts.
They can create prompts on off.
Our prompt generation platform starts to seed it.
And there's quite a few capabilities here.
Filter by branded versus non-branded prompts.
Filter by your personas.
Filter by the key topics.
Filter by each platform.
Filter by stage of the customer journey.
Right.
So it's like if I'm in the.
Branded in here as well.
Let me cut up for a little bit, Chris.
Chris.
He's He's it.
He's He's He's He's got
How would they know the stage of the customer journey?
It's just based on an intent analysis of the prompt.
So like depending on what the prompt is, same way SEMrush would do search intent for keywords, right?
Okay, it's not like linked to a CRM or something and mapping.
No, no, no, no.
You're coming back?
Can guys hear me now?
Yeah, you're back.
I don't know what's better, me thinking the app crashed or losing Wi-Fi.
We lost Wi-Fi that time.
Wait, quick question.
Like how, like for using the platform, is it always kind of like, I have data from when I start tracking or do you like pool that a little bit?

I mean, I know you don't have that many customers, so maybe this is like a future thing, but like, like if I pick a prompt, I know for the last year, you know, what it's, what is performance and kind of.
Yeah, we're not, we're not doing any pooling right now across customers.
So literally from the day you start, so there's no sharing across, across customer environments.
sense.
back.
Which is good and bad, right?
It's like, I think it's good because it's unique to them, but it also is we do not have historical data for an entity that comes in.
We've talked about creating some, like, industry slices of, like, you're in the CMS space, here's the things we monitor, you're in the...
Yeah, mean, it seems like there's, like, a pool around just tools, like, that tool for X or Y that kind of would be interesting to keep, but, yeah.
Yeah.
But you get the sense here, right?
So I was flipping through stages of the customer journey.
I I flip to comparison, obviously Disney's in that prompt, so they're going to show up 100% of the time.
If I flip to all branded and non-branded and move to, like, the awareness stage, they're going to show up a little bit less frequently.
This is, like, I'm looking for something in the subscription category, but I don't know what it is.
And so, you know, Netflix is showing up a little more highly.
Okay.
I mean, I know you have hard stuff, so maybe we can at least talk about next steps a little bit.
I think, like, I like how clean your product is.
Because profound seems a little bit more, like, opinionated.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And, I don't know, there's just something, like, sleek and, like, you know, weird about it, but I like Scrunch.
Like, what could we get access to to keep exploring this?
Do you have the pricing that you could share with us?
Kirkland's the one on our side that's kind of, like, building all this, so, like, if could get access to something to kind of dig a little bit deeper, that would be great.
Yeah.
Yeah, why don't we do, like, week access or something, Kirkland, for you, so you can kind of play around, set up one environment.
We'll do a kickoff with Will on our customer success and data team.
But I ultimately think, like, you may even want to think a little bit about, like, what endpoints you want from an API perspective.
So, you know, we touched on, let me just click on, like, one area here to give you a little bit more depth of, like, what you'd be pulling back from us, right?
So, if you're looking at a prompt, you you can filter between every one of the models.
You can see what web.
Sites powered the answer.
You can see presence, position, sentiment, and you can create prompts on the fly, right?
So if want to ask a new question, do it, tie it to a persona, tag it.

And then the last section, the API perspective that's pretty critical is this sources capability, which is mapping all of the website's own competitive and third party that are in AI answers with, is your brand present?
What competitors are present?
What prompts is it tied to?
So when you think about distribution and like citations as maybe the new backlink, it's like, okay, is the brand present here?
Yes or no.
Is it our site?
Is it a competitive site?
Is it a third party site?
What's the sentiment?
So we think position, presence, sentiment, citations.
Those are the things that I think you'd be taking into a Looker Studio environment and then visualizing for dashboards at scale for customers.

So why don't we set you up next week to play with it for a week, just like in the interface, but then move into more discussions on consuming an API because that's kind of the end game if we move forward.
That sounds great.
And then from my end, I think just on the partnership level, I know you guys are working with Daydream.
It's critical for me that whoever we use here, this partnership, I want it to go deeper.
When people think of AISCO, I want them to stop.
I don't even know who they think about today, but I want that to be us and whoever that partner is.
And again, know you work with Daydream, so maybe this is something we can speak about a little bit more.
But I want webinars, reports, just deep thought in the space.
I want it to be Graphite, Daydream, whatever.
I want it to be us and whoever we partner with.
Yeah, I would love to talk more about that.
Because the data, to your point, is commoditized, right?
I think this brand is important.
Super open to doing joint webinars, joint white papers.
Would definitely not insist on that being branded across our client base.
It's like unique efforts that we take with you, we would do separately.
Obviously, we're not going to be exclusive in who is consuming our API.
If they want to take insights and publish it, great, they can go do that.
But distribution is critical for us.
So if we have an opportunity to speak publicly about insights, or do webinars together, we're certainly open to it.
Okay.
And how big are you guys at this point?
How do we define big people?
People.
I guess people.
And then you guys are seed stage.
I think I already saw that.
Yeah, I guess the point is just people.
And if you can't tell, like a rough size of like, I mean, you said 60 customers, so I can probably just guess, but maybe just people.
We're not sharing any revenue numbers yet.
We've been super lean.
So we did a $4 million seed run with Mayfield.
We will be like 11 people at the end of April here.
We were three people two months ago.
So like we stayed really lean through the first year building.
And one of the benefits of building again is like head of sales.
I worked with for five years, head of growth and market.
I worked with five years.
My co-founder, we did 12 years at the last company.
We spent 12 years together, sold it to Yex, 300 people, 60 in revenue.
We know how to build platforms and teams for the long haul.
And so I think measuring people was a weird thing in today's environment, given how efficient you can build technology and scale things.
But that gives you a little sense of kind of where we are.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And then on your team, are you the person that's thinking like the deepest about like brand and marketing and kind of like how your go-to-market works out?
Yes and no.
mean, my attention is definitely split.
I've hired a head of marketing and growth, this woman named Colleen, that would certainly be involved in kind of like joint press and marketing and thought leadership webinars.
So like she would help spearhead that effort.
I'd probably be speaking along with the representative from the partner.
But I have a woman running marketing and growth on the team.
Sounds good.
Great to chat, guys.
Thanks, Chris.
Kirkland, we'll connect next week with Will.
Yeah, that sounds great.
Thanks, guys.-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential partnership between GrowthX and Scrunch AI for AI-powered SEO monitoring and insights.](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=147.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Scrunch AI offers comprehensive AI search monitoring across multiple platforms (GPT, Claude, Perplexity, etc.)](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=411.0)
  - [GrowthX is interested in integrating AI search data into client reporting and exploring deeper partnership opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=304.0)
  - [Scrunch provides API access for partners to integrate data into custom dashboards](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
  - [Both parties expressed interest in potential co-marketing and thought leadership collaborations](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=851.0)

## Topics

### Scrunch AI's Offering

  - [Monitoring is viewed as a "commodity" wedge into more valuable capabilities](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=442.0)
  - [Tracks presence, position, sentiment, and citations across AI search platforms](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=589.0)
  - [Persona-based approach for generating relevant prompts and analyzing results](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=488.0)
  - [API access available for partners to integrate data into custom dashboards](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
  - [Pricing based on volume of prompts/entities monitored, with custom options for partners](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=363.0)

### GrowthX's Needs and Interests

  - [Seeking AI search data to integrate into client reporting (potentially via Looker Studio)](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=182.0)
  - [Interested in comprehensive attribution tracking from AI mentions to referral visits](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=204.0)
  - [Looking for a partner to establish thought leadership in AI SEO space](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=816.0)
  - [Exploring options before building in-house solution](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=319.0)

### Potential Partnership Structure

  - [API access for GrowthX to integrate Scrunch data into client dashboards](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1704.0)
  - [Possible co-marketing opportunities (webinars, whitepapers, etc.)](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1851.0)
  - [Mutual referral agreement for customer introductions](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
  - [Non-exclusive partnership, allowing both to work with other players in the space](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1863.0)

### Market Landscape and Challenges

  - [Rapidly evolving space with many new entrants in past 4-5 months](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=437.0)
  - [Difficulty in accurately measuring AI search volume and user behavior](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=876.0)
  - [Potential shift in web content creation to prioritize AI crawlers over human readers](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Set up 1-week trial access for GrowthX team to explore Scrunch platform](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1704.0)
  - [Schedule follow-up call to discuss API integration possibilities](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1788.0)
  - [Explore co-marketing and thought leadership collaboration opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1851.0)
  - [Continue discussions on partnership structure and pricing models](https://fathom.video/share/gAsWLWzJRtY76PXHXv4gXGwfEf7gFneb?tab=summary&timestamp=1788.0)

action_items.description: Set up 1-week Scrunch platform access for Kirkland
Connect w/ Will (Scrunch) next week for platform kickoff
Follow up w/ Chris re: deeper partnership opps (webinars, reports)
Discuss potential joint marketing efforts w/ Colleen (head of marketing)
